title: Parametrized Variational Quantum Tomography
authors: V. A. Penas, M. Losada, D. Tielas, F. Holik
abstract: Quantum state tomography provides a fundamental framework for reconstructing quantum states. When the measurement data are not informationally complete, the observed statistics admit multiple compatible density matrices, making the reconstruction problem inherently underdetermined and calling for the selection of a meaningful estimator. Two well-established approaches to address this ambiguity are Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) and Variational Quantum Tomography (VQT). A variant of VQT, named VQT$_\infty$, has been introduced to reproduce MaxEnt-like solutions. In this work, we generalize this approach by introducing a parametrized cost function that interpolates between the 1-norm and the infinity norm, thereby unifying VQT and VQT$_\infty$ within a single framework. By tuning the associated hyperparameters, the proposed method enables controlled exploration of the set of compatible density matrices. We show that this interplay yields reconstructed states with higher fidelity to the MaxEnt solution than those obtained via VQT$_\infty$ while preserving computational tractability.
summary: The authors propose a new method for quantum state tomography that unifies different variational approaches using a parametrized cost function. By tuning hyperparameters, the method can achieve results closer to Maximum Entropy solutions while remaining computationally efficient. This allows for better reconstruction of quantum states when measurement data is incomplete.
main_problem: Addressing the underdetermined nature of quantum state tomography when measurement data is not informationally complete.
main_result: The introduction of a unified framework that interpolates between the 1-norm and infinity norm, allowing for a controlled exploration of compatible density matrices with higher fidelity to MaxEnt solutions.
method: A parametrized cost function that generalizes Variational Quantum Tomography (VQT) and VQT-infinity by interpolating between different norms.
model_or_system: The paper focuses on the general problem of quantum state reconstruction from incomplete measurement statistics.
key_observables: Quantum state fidelity and measurement statistics.
important_parameters: Hyperparameters associated with the interpolated cost function that control the transition between 1-norm and infinity norm.
main_assumptions: The measurement data is not informationally complete, leading to multiple compatible density matrices.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of underdetermined tomography, discusses existing MaxEnt and VQT approaches, proposes a new interpolated cost function, and demonstrates its performance relative to VQT-infinity.
why_interesting: It provides a new tool for more accurate quantum state reconstruction in scenarios with limited measurement capabilities, which is critical for characterizing noisy quantum devices.
